general_education.framework: United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs)

first_year_foundation.name: Bryant IDEA (Innovation and Design Experience For All)

mathematics.name: Statistics I (or MATH 202 Biostatistics / MATH 203 Sports Statistics)

economics.name: Macroeconomic Principles
economics.note: Required for College of Business; Arts & Sciences may substitute second social science

core_disciplines_12cr.options: Anthropology, Language Studies, Legal Studies, Political Science, Psychology, or Sociology
Biology, Geology, Physics, Chemistry, Climate Change, Ecology, or related

communication.department: Department of Communication and Language Studies

courses.name: Health Communication, Identity and Sport
courses.description: Examines relationships between health communication and public health in athletic contexts, focusing on mental health, identity issues among college athletes, and wellness representation in sports.

courses.name: Public Speaking
courses.description: Develops skills in researching, organizing, and delivering public messages across informative, persuasive, and entertainment formats, with emphasis on audience analysis and managing communication anxiety.

courses.name: Introduction to Communication
courses.description: Explores communication definitions, research methods, and major theories. Introduces students to various specializations including interpersonal, mass, health, intercultural, and small group communication.

courses.name: Introduction to Digital Communication
courses.description: Surveys the evolution of digital communication technologies and their impact on human interaction, examining internet governance, ethics, privacy, and industries transformed by digital innovation.

courses.name: Introduction to Sports Communication
courses.description: Overview of sport communication including media, advertising, public relations, and employment opportunities, with practical experience in press conferences and sports reporting.

courses.name: Esports Communication and Media Studies
courses.description: Introduces communication's role in esports through media relations, broadcasting, content creation, and branding analysis across digital platforms.

courses.name: Principles of Visual Communication in the Digital Space
courses.description: Examines foundational design theories including composition, color, typography, and visual hierarchy, analyzing how visual elements influence audience perception and engagement.

courses.name: Introduction to Film Studies
courses.description: Investigates film language, cinematography, production design, and cultural representation, examining how movies reflect and shape societal perceptions regarding identity and social issues.

courses.name: Basic Studio Production
courses.description: Introduces television production fundamentals through studio exercises, emphasizing how software and hardware structure visual, auditory, and motion elements for video communication.

courses.name: Digital Media Production
courses.description: Teaches single-camera video production and Adobe Premiere editing through hands-on projects, focusing on shot composition, camera movement, and storytelling techniques.

courses.name: Media Literacy
courses.description: Provides tools for critically examining mass media content including advertisements, television, and digital platforms, addressing misinformation and evaluating media's cultural impact.

courses.name: Public Relations and Social Media
courses.description: Introduces PR concepts and social media strategy, teaching students to develop communication plans integrating traditional and digital channels for organizational objectives.

courses.name: Interpersonal Communication
courses.description: Examines communication elements affecting relationships including language, perception, nonverbal signals, and conflict, applying research to real-world social interactions.

courses.name: Mass Communication
courses.description: Familiarizes students with media industries' structures, economics, and content, emphasizing media literacy, ownership concentration, and cultural responsibility.

courses.name: Introduction to Health Communication
courses.description: Introduces health communication foundations including patient-provider interactions, health literacy, campaigns, technology's influence, and cultural factors in healthcare messaging.

courses.name: Esports Governance and Policy
courses.description: Examines esports leagues, teams, and governance structures, exploring legal frameworks, ethical standards, and stakeholder roles in competitive gaming ecosystems.

courses.name: Sports Broadcasting and Analysis
courses.description: Develops practical skills for sports media careers through hands-on projects, teaching commentary techniques, on-air presence, and industry navigation strategies.

courses.name: Writing and Reporting for Broadcast and Digital Media
courses.description: Teaches broadcast journalism skills including researching, writing news stories, conducting interviews, and delivering content for television and internet platforms.

courses.name: Public Health Communication: Advocacy and Action
courses.description: Examines public health history and current issues, teaching social marketing, media advocacy, risk communication, and qualitative research methods for health initiatives.

courses.name: Sports Media Production
courses.description: Uses live sports broadcasting as a platform for teaching multi-camera directing, field production, editing, writing, and rapid editorial decision-making.

courses.name: Documentary Filmmaking
courses.description: Teaches nonfiction film creation including topic selection, quasi-narrative frameworks, directing, writing, and editing while managing unpredictable real-world production challenges.

courses.name: Talk Radio: Sports, Politics and Podcasting
courses.description: Develops skills for talk radio and podcast hosting, emphasizing research, interviewing, storytelling, and integrating emerging broadcast technologies.

courses.name: Strategic Content Planning and Creation
courses.description: Explores strategies for building audience-organization relationships through audits, style guides, briefs, and authentic social media content curation across platforms.

courses.name: Writing for Digital Media
courses.description: Teaches students to create, analyze, and compose digital content across multiple genres, tools, platforms, and audiences using multimodal writing processes.

courses.name: Digital Video and Media Narrative Design
courses.description: Focuses on digital storytelling skills for producing, writing, capturing, and editing content across various purposes and platforms.

courses.name: The Sociological Imagination: What We See When We Watch T.V.
courses.description: Applies sociological analysis to television, examining character narratives and urban spaces to explore work, morality, gender, race, and social justice themes.

courses.name: Crisis and Risk Communication
courses.description: Examines crisis and risk communication processes, planning, and implementation strategies for organizational scandals, environmental disasters, and public health hazards.

courses.name: Public Relations
courses.description: Examines the PR process, corporate-public relationships, media methods, and legal/ethical issues while developing programs and communications for various media channels.

courses.name: Conflict Management and Negotiation
courses.description: Introduces conflict study and communication's role in causing, escalating, and managing disputes, exploring negotiation within intimate, workplace, and team contexts.

courses.name: Small Group Communication
courses.description: Surveys small group theory including cohesiveness, conflict, power, roles, and decision-making, with practical application to problem-solving group environments.

courses.name: Media Organizations
courses.description: Addresses management, production, and distribution of mass media, examining organizational history, business aspects, regulation, and technology's impact.

courses.name: Nonverbal Communication
courses.description: In-depth study of nonverbal codes including body language, eye contact, and vocalics, examining their communicative functions and role in overall messaging.

courses.name: Message Design and Content Creation in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
courses.description: Explores AI's transformative role in communication, teaching message design, audience analysis, and content creation while addressing authenticity and ethical AI use.

courses.name: Research Methods in Communication/Digital Communication
courses.description: Introduces research methods including ethical implications, sampling, variables, hypothesis testing, and statistical analysis for communication research and evaluation.

courses.name: Communication Internship
courses.description: Supervised work experience applying communication skills and theory to professional environments.

courses.name: Advanced Intercultural Communication
courses.description: Explores how culture influences communication through theoretical and practical approaches, examining context's role in intercultural interactions across education, healthcare, and business.

courses.name: Digital Literacy, Digital Lives
courses.description: Develops critical thinking skills for assessing, analyzing, and creating digital content while examining digital technology's impact on interactions, society, and relationships.

courses.name: Organization, Leadership, and Communication in the Sports Industry
courses.description: Focuses on organizational structures and communication requirements for sports entities, examining departments, roles, interdependence, and coordination.

courses.name: Esports Event Planning and Organization
courses.description: Explores planning and managing esports events from grassroots to international tournaments, covering logistics, sponsorship, audience engagement, and tournament technologies.

courses.name: Advanced Sports Broadcasting: Road to Radio Row
courses.description: Provides immersive experience in live sports media production with culminating participation in Super Bowl Radio Row.

courses.name: Voices of the Game: Expert Perspectives on Sports Communication
courses.description: Features accomplished athletes and sports executives sharing expertise on communication, media, sponsorship, fan engagement, and leadership.

courses.name: Advanced Digital Media
courses.description: Examines digital platform production across traditional and emerging services, teaching content creation from preproduction through distribution.

courses.name: Sports Journalism
courses.description: Prepares students for sports coverage across multiple formats through hands-on instruction in researching, interviewing, writing, and developing unique journalistic voices.

courses.name: Media Genre Studies
courses.description: Examines media genres as dynamic industry-audience exchanges, analyzing how categorization reveals production institutions, market demands, and historical moments.

courses.name: Personal Branding in Digital Media
courses.description: Explores branding history and self-presentation in digital contexts, emphasizing content creation for digital platforms to strengthen personal and professional brand images.

courses.name: The Impact of Digital Addiction on Individuals and Social Interactions
courses.description: Examines digital communication's effects on cognitive processes, addiction, and culture, exploring behavioral engineering, memes, identity, and mental health implications.

courses.name: Persuasion and Social Influence
courses.description: Introduces theoretical and applied issues in social influence, examining persuasive message creation and consumption across advertising, politics, health, and relationships.

courses.name: Media Effects
courses.description: Examines mass media's impact including cultivation, desensitization, agenda-setting, violence, and gender portrayals, requiring semester-long research projects.

courses.name: The Dark Side of Human Communication
courses.description: Investigates difficult social interactions including jealousy, deception, infidelity, stalking, and breakups, applying research and theory.

courses.name: Global Communication
courses.description: Uses comparative approaches to examine cross-national communication policy and practice regarding broadcasting, telecommunications, and internet culture.

courses.name: Advanced Health Communication Health Campaigns
courses.description: In-depth study of health message design, promotion, and behavior change through theories, models, and current campaigns.

courses.name: Senior Capstone for Communication/Digital Communication
courses.description: Synthesizes communication theory and ethics through real-world application, requiring students to solve nonprofit communication problems.

courses.name: Senior Capstone in Sports Industries, Media, and Promotion
courses.description: Applies sports communication theories and concepts to real industry problems.

courses.name: Directed Study in Communication
courses.description: Permits independent pursuit of communication interests under faculty supervision.

psychology.college: School of Health and Behavioral Sciences

courses.name: Introduction to Psychology
courses.description: Addresses major principles, theories and research methods for understanding mental processing and behavior, surveying human behavior across contexts with ethical considerations.

courses.name: Honors: Core Concepts in Psychology
courses.description: Covers the same foundational material as PSY 260 with opportunities for honors students to contribute directly to teaching course content.

courses.name: Sensation and Perception
courses.description: Explores biological and psychological processes of perception, sensory systems, brain structures processing sensory information, and how learning augments perception.

courses.name: Psychology of Personality
courses.description: Examines historical and contemporary approaches to personality understanding, incorporating cross-cultural and gender influences with applications to case studies.

courses.name: Introduction to Psychopathology
courses.description: Emphasizes contemporary approaches to understanding causes and treatments of psychological and psychiatric disorders.

courses.name: Child and Adolescent Development
courses.description: Examines human development from prenatal period through adolescence using current research methods and relevant theories.

courses.name: Adult Development and Aging
courses.description: Investigates psychological and physical changes throughout adulthood with emphasis on aging experiences in modern society.

courses.name: Environmental Psychology
courses.description: Uses interdisciplinary perspective to investigate environmental effects on behavior and human performance.

courses.name: Applied Psychology
courses.description: Addresses practical applications of psychological research to real-world problems across biomedical, educational, industrial/organizational, sports, legal, and other contexts.

courses.name: Positive Psychology
courses.description: Focuses on subjective well-being, optimal human functioning, positive traits, and collective societal wellbeing across the lifespan.

courses.name: Cognitive Psychology
courses.description: Overviews primary cognitive psychology areas including neuroscience, perception, attention, memory, language, decision-making, and problem-solving.

courses.name: Introduction to Neuroscience
courses.description: Foundational overview of nervous system structure and function, including neuronal communication, brain organization, sensory systems, and neural basis of behavior.

courses.name: Health Psychology
courses.description: Overviews health psychology areas including stress, illness, coping, lifestyle enhancement, chronic illness, pain, and life-threatening health problems.

courses.name: Research Methods in Psychology
courses.description: Introduces experimental methods focusing on research planning, experimental design, statistics, APA-style report writing, and critical evaluation of scientific evidence.

courses.name: Educational Psychology
courses.description: Explores psychological principles and theories applied to teaching and learning, covering learning theories, motivation, development, individual differences, and assessment.

courses.name: Industrial and Organization Psychology
courses.description: Focuses on human behavior in the workplace, addressing hiring, management, employee development, retention, productivity, and employee wellbeing.

courses.name: Neural Circuits and Systems
courses.description: Examines how neural circuits produce sensation, action, attention, executive function, language, emotion, motivation, and sleep, exploring circuit disruptions in mental illness.

courses.name: Research Methods in Psychology II
courses.description: Second research methods course emphasizing research design, implementation, data analysis, and interpretation for psychology majors.

courses.name: Psychology Internship
courses.description: Supervised work-study applying psychological theory in work environments (120 hours minimum).

courses.name: Neurobiology of Learning and Memory
courses.description: Explores current understanding of learning and memory processes from human and animal research, examining measurement, different memory systems, and underlying mechanisms.

courses.name: The Design Thinking Process
courses.description: Hands-on course applying design thinking while developing understanding of cognitive and behavioral principles underlying innovation and problem-solving.

courses.name: Cross-Cultural Psychology
courses.description: In-depth examination of culture's role in socialization and behavior, addressing cross-cultural psychology methodology.

courses.name: Social Psychology
courses.description: Examines factors impacting human relationships including interpersonal attraction, attitudes, social perception, altruism, aggression, group behavior, and social influence.

courses.name: Gender in Childhood
courses.description: Addresses gender meaning and its effects on children's experiences and identities, considering biological and socio-cultural factors.

courses.name: Child Psychopathology
courses.description: Focuses on atypical childhood development, covering defining characteristics, causes, diagnosis, and intervention approaches for behavioral, mood, and developmental disorders.

courses.name: Counseling Theory and Practice
courses.description: Reviews major contemporary counseling theories and techniques with opportunities for observation and practice, addressing cross-cultural issues.

courses.name: Exercise and Sport Psychology
courses.description: Studies how psychology promotes and maintains exercise and sport behavior across the lifespan with emphasis on practical applications.

courses.name: Forensic Psychology
courses.description: Introduces forensic psychology covering criminal profiling, hypnosis, lie detection, eyewitness testimony, jury selection, insanity defense, and abuse cases.

courses.name: Drugs and Behavior
courses.description: Overviews topics related to drugs and behavioral effects, covering historical perspectives, drug action, neurobiology, and psychopharmacology.

courses.name: Psychological Testing and Assessment
courses.description: Explores assessment goals and principles, measurement theory, test construction, major test types, and contemporary issues including bias and ethical concerns.

courses.name: Judgement and Decision Making
courses.description: Examines human judgment and decision-making research with applications to health, financial, legal, political, and personal relationship decisions.

courses.name: Senior Research Seminar
courses.description: Integrates accumulated knowledge through development and investigation of applied psychology hypotheses, culminating in written and oral presentations.

courses.name: Senior Internship Seminar
courses.description: Integrates and applies prior coursework through diverse field placements combined with classroom seminars.

courses.name: Directed Study in Psychology
courses.description: Independent and in-depth study of specific psychology topics through individually supervised research projects.

history.department: Department of History, Literature, and the Arts

courses.name: Emergence of Europe (1000-1600)
courses.description: Examines the origins and early development of Europe from 1000-1600, covering feudalism, national monarchies, urbanism and society during the Renaissance and Reformation.

courses.name: Europe: 1500 to 1815
courses.description: Study of political, intellectual, and socio-economic history of early modern Europe including the Reformation, Scientific Revolution, and English & French revolutions.

courses.name: History of the United States to 1877
courses.description: Basic survey of American history covering political, cultural and economic development of the United States through Reconstruction.

courses.name: History of the United States Since 1865
courses.description: History of the American experience from end of Reconstruction to present, focusing on Urban-Industrial age, rise of US to world leadership, and changes in American life.

courses.name: American Women's History
courses.description: Surveys American women's history from colonial times to present, examining how major developments affected women and the lives of ordinary and leading women thinkers and activists.

courses.name: World History to 1500
courses.description: Traces development of humanity and society from dawn of history to 1500, providing insight into ideas, institutions, and life practices across different cultures.

courses.name: World History Since 1500
courses.description: Historical study of major regions and cultures of the world during the last five centuries, with attention to connections, interactions, and global trends.

courses.name: Introduction to Latin American History
courses.description: Basic survey from pre-European invasion to recent times, emphasizing diversity of the Latin American experience alongside historical continuities of conflict, inequality, and imperial domination.

courses.name: History in the World Today
courses.description: Develops argumentation skills regarding complex historical problems while strengthening global perspective through analyzing current events' historical dimensions.

courses.name: Introduction to American Studies
courses.description: Introduces key American Studies themes and concepts through methodology enabling interpretation of literary texts, films, documents, music, visual art, and popular culture.

courses.name: To Arms! History of Modern France
courses.description: Examines French social and political evolution from the eighteenth century through WWI, emphasizing the Revolution, Napoleonic era, imperial expansion, and cultural movements.

courses.name: French Studies
courses.description: Biocultural approach to French philosophical thought across centuries, examining works by major thinkers. Topics may include humanism, skepticism, feminism, and constructivism.

courses.name: Italian Studies
courses.description: Focuses on Italian history, society, politics, culture, and economics. Rotating topics may address humanism, science, fascism, and the Vatican.

courses.name: History of Modern Europe: 1815 to the Present
courses.description: Examines major political, economic, and intellectual developments since 1815, emphasizing significant events, patterns, and themes in Western history.

courses.name: Trends in Modern Thought
courses.description: Selected history of modern and post-modern themes and ideologies in Euro-America since the Renaissance, with social, political, and philosophical focus.

courses.name: Gender and World War II
courses.description: Explores whether WWII served as a transformative force for women globally, examining diverse socioeconomic and ethnic perspectives across US, Europe, and Asia.

courses.name: The United States in the 1960s
courses.description: Examines 1960s political, economic, social, and cultural contours, addressing Civil Rights Movement, Vietnam, conservatism's resurgence, and cultural expressions.

courses.name: History of American Technology
courses.description: Treats technology history within American business and social contexts, focusing on 19th and 20th centuries and technological impact on institutions, business, and culture.

courses.name: The United States and World Politics, 1890 to the Present
courses.description: Examines American emergence as a great power from the Spanish-American War forward, evaluating US global role.

courses.name: Race in America
courses.description: Examines race relations from slavery's onset to present, addressing slavery origins, immigration, abolitionism, Jim Crow, Civil Rights Movement, and post-civil rights era.

courses.name: The History of American Popular Culture
courses.description: Explores American popular culture expressions across media including literature, film, music, and advertising, examining relationships to economics and social developments.

courses.name: Gender and American Culture in the 1950s
courses.description: Examines 1950s cultural complexities through television and literature, showing how women challenged domestic stereotypes.

courses.name: U.S. Latin American Relations 1820 to Present
courses.description: Examines historical relations between the United States and Latin American nations from the Monroe Doctrine era to contemporary times.

courses.name: History of Russia
courses.description: Historical study of Russian societal evolution from the Age of Kiev through Soviet and contemporary periods.

courses.name: History of East Asia
courses.description: Historical examination of East Asian ideas and institutions, emphasizing China's ancient and modern development since 1800, plus contemporary regional problems.

courses.name: History of Modern Africa
courses.description: Provides context for analyzing major contemporary African challenges through coverage of ancient culture, slavery, colonialism, nationalism, and modern trends.

courses.name: History of Modern Japan
courses.description: Surveys Japanese history from origins through 20th century, addressing political, social, economic, intellectual, and cultural developments.

courses.name: Doing Public History
courses.description: Enables students to examine local history and communicate findings publicly while engaging debates about whose history merits documentation.

courses.name: History, Law, and the Holocaust
courses.description: In-depth Holocaust exploration examining anti-Semitism, Nazi rise, and post-1945 international law development, concluding with contemporary human rights law.

courses.name: History Internship
courses.description: Supervised work-study arrangement applying history theory and principles in professional settings.

courses.name: Buy American: Consumer Culture in U.S. History
courses.description: Examines American consumer culture's rise from 19th through 21st centuries using interdisciplinary case studies addressing mass marketing, advertising, and consumer activism.

courses.name: World War I: Causes, Courses, and Consequences
courses.description: Examines the seminal 20th-century conflict, analyzing political-cultural-military factors, wartime experiences, peacemaking, and enduring legacy.

courses.name: The World Since 1945
courses.description: Examines major post-1945 global developments including capitalist and socialist systems, the Cold War, imperialism, independence movements, and the new world order.

courses.name: History of Modern Britain
courses.description: Traces British history from 1688 through present, examining capitalism, constitutionalism, industrialism, and imperialism's impacts.

courses.name: History of Modern Science
courses.description: Presents natural sciences history from 18th through 20th centuries, focusing on conceptual problems and scientific culture.

courses.name: Foundations of the Modern Middle East
courses.description: Foundational Middle East understanding through Abrahamic traditions, Islamic history, Ottoman Empire, and British Palestine mandate, concluding with Arab-Israeli conflict.

courses.name: History of Contemporary America
courses.description: Intensive examination of forces shaping recent America, emphasizing domestic politics, social change, urbanization, civil rights and ecological problems.

courses.name: The United States in the 1970s and 1980s
courses.description: Explores 1970s-1980s shifts including conservatism's ascendancy, economic inequality, social movements, and media-politics relationships.

courses.name: Seminar in Historical Inquiry
courses.description: Senior capstone with extensive practical experience in historical research and writing while examining historiographical and philosophical debates.

courses.name: Directed Study in History
courses.description: Independent study under faculty direction requiring substantial papers or projects.

courses.name: Special Topics: Modern American Civil Rights Movement
courses.description: Traces Black freedom struggle from the 1950s-1970s examining organizational, leadership, and ideological aspects.

courses.name: Special Topics in Native American History
courses.description: Introduces Indigenous North American history from pre-contact through present using Indigenous perspectives.

courses.name: Special Topics: Race and Slavery in the Atlantic World
courses.description: Examines 15th through 19th-century Atlantic World slavery and race, emphasizing trans-Atlantic trade impacts.

courses.name: Special Topics: The Space Race: A History
courses.description: Examines 20th-century manned space flight evolution, focusing on Cold War rivalry and the African-American women integral to orbital dynamics.

courses.name: Special Topics: War Crimes in World History
courses.description: Explores global war crimes history and legal responses, tracing human efforts limiting warfare from antiquity through modern criminal trials.

courses.name: Principles of Sociology
courses.description: Students survey and appraise the basic concepts including theory and method, social processes and structure.

courses.name: Honors Sociology
courses.description: Advanced introduction to sociology exploring social theory, research methods, social structure, culture, groups, socialization, social interaction, inequality, and social institutions.

courses.name: Social Problems Social Solutions
courses.description: Examines major contemporary social problems and their explanations, assessing effectiveness of solutions regarding race, ethnicity, gender, immigration, education, age, criminal justice, overpopulation, and environmental degradation.

courses.name: Sociology of Gender, Illness, and Health
courses.description: Explores gender's role in health opportunities and illness likelihood, examining different health outcomes for women and men.

courses.name: Globalization and Childhood
courses.description: Examines impacts of globalization on children and childhood across the globe, including children as producers and consumers.

courses.name: Social Determinants of Health
courses.description: Examines conditions and systems influencing health outcomes through structural determinants, economic policies, and discrimination using medical anthropology perspectives.

courses.name: Sociology of Family
courses.description: Explores American family changes, mythology of traditional families, diversity of current family forms, examining hooking up, partnering, parenting, household labor division, and divorce.

courses.name: The Sociological Imagination: What We See When We Watch T.V.
courses.description: Applies sociological imagination lens to television content analysis examining character lives, urban space, work, morality, sexuality, politics, and social justice.

courses.name: Sociology of Sport
courses.description: Overview of sociology of sport, focusing on global aspects and relationship between sport and race, class, gender.

courses.name: Sociology of Innovation and Creativity
courses.description: Takes sociological perspective on creativity and innovation exploring the sociological context of each, with community-based projects.

courses.name: Crime and Justice
courses.description: Applies sociological theory and research to the study of crime and social control, with policy debates and research projects.

courses.name: Research Methods in Sociology
courses.description: Introduces systematic strategies to gather, analyze, and interpret sociological data via survey research, including SPSS statistical analysis.

courses.name: Sociology Internship
courses.description: Individually supervised work-study arrangements applying social science theory and principles.

courses.name: Population and Society
courses.description: Examines determinants and consequences of population processes, exploring relevance to economic development, gender equality, immigration, poverty, healthcare, and environmental issues.

courses.name: Sociology of Work
courses.description: Exploration of the meaning of work in our lives and culture, challenging assumptions about social organization of work.

courses.name: Race and Ethnicity
courses.description: Examines theories to understand treatment and life chances of racial and ethnic subpopulations.

courses.name: Social Theory: The Study of Isms and Phobias
courses.description: Examines classical and contemporary social theory to understand inequalities including racism, sexism, classism, heterosexism.

courses.name: Urban Sociology
courses.description: Uses urban theory to analyze the relationship between larger social forces and individual experiences, focusing on city formation and urbanism's effects.

courses.name: Applied Seminar in Social Entrepreneurship
courses.description: Background in social entrepreneurship with hands-on experience creating solutions to significant global problems.

courses.name: Sociology Capstone I
courses.description: Senior-level capstone seminar exploring an important social issue as it relates to social institutions.

courses.name: Sociology Capstone II
courses.description: Continuation of SOC 491 providing opportunity for extended, in-depth research.

courses.name: Directed Study in Sociology
courses.description: Independent, in-depth study or research under faculty direction with substantial paper or project.

courses.name: Special Topics: From Womb to Tomb - Sociology of Sexuality
courses.description: Examines how sexuality is culturally, politically, and legally defined throughout the life cycle.

courses.name: Special Topics: From Asylums to Zoloft - Sociology of Mental Health
courses.description: Explores social dimensions of mental health and illness, examining historical and cultural definitions and modern trends' effects on wellbeing.

courses.name: Contemporary Problems and Policy Responses
courses.description: Places students in a dynamic learning environment wrestling with complex, real-world problems. Emphasizes problem-solving skills, critical thinking, and ethical reasoning.

courses.name: Introduction to International Politics
courses.description: Introduction to international relations covering nation-state development, war and peace, human rights, and economic/environmental policies. Explores international institutions like the UN and World Bank.

courses.name: Introduction to U.S. Politics
courses.description: Surveys U.S. government topics including the Constitution, Congress, presidency, Supreme Court, federalism, political parties, elections, interest groups, civil liberties, and civil rights.

courses.name: Honors Politics of the International System
courses.description: Explores the current international political system, historical developments, competing IR theories, and contemporary global issues.

courses.name: Honors Contemporary U.S. Politics
courses.description: Covers ideas and historical factors shaping the U.S. political system, including institutions, parties, elections, and contemporary politics.

courses.name: United States Foreign Policy
courses.description: Surveys instruments, implementation, and issues of U.S. foreign policy, America's rise to power, and post-WWII overseas involvements.

courses.name: American Political Economy
courses.description: Examines relationships between government and business, politics and economy, and power and money in the United States.

courses.name: Political Parties and Elections
courses.description: Covers party history, organization, nominations, elections, voting, and roles of pressure groups, public opinion, and media.

courses.name: The U.S. Congress
courses.description: Examines Congress as a central institution, analyzing elections, internal organization, committees, legislative processes, and relationships with other branches.

courses.name: The Presidency in Modern American Politics
courses.description: Explores how the presidency transformed from the Constitution's outline to today's centerpiece of American politics.

courses.name: Comparative Politics
courses.description: Examines key concepts and trends using comparative methodology across democracies, authoritarian regimes, communist systems, and developing countries.

courses.name: Politics of Asia
courses.description: Analyzes government and domestic politics of India, China, and Japan using comparative methodology.

courses.name: Global Environmental Sustainability and Policy
courses.description: Examines intersection of environmental sustainability, political policy, and economic issues with focus on climate change.

courses.name: Political Science Internship
courses.description: Supervised work-study arrangement applying political science theory.

courses.name: Interest Groups and Lobbying
courses.description: Examines interest groups and lobbyists in American politics, analyzing organization, campaign finance, policy influence, and social movements.

courses.name: International Security
courses.description: Analyzes the modern international system and power struggles, including balance of power, war and peace, national power, international law, and diplomacy.

courses.name: Ethics in International Affairs
courses.description: Examines ethical constraints on force use, human rights norms, global inequality, development programs, and ethical implications of the global economy.

courses.name: Political Ideologies - Old and New
courses.description: Examines liberalism, conservatism, and Marxism plus modern ideological debates over nationalism, environmentalism, and feminism.

courses.name: Politics of Development
courses.description: Examines political, economic, and social structures of developing countries in Asia, Africa, Latin America, and Middle East.

courses.name: Politics of International Economic Relations
courses.description: Focuses on governments and international economic organizations examining conflict/cooperation on trade, investment, exchange rates, and multinational corporations.

courses.name: Seminar in Politics and Law
courses.description: Interdisciplinary capstone for Politics and Law majors with in-depth examination and major research projects.

courses.name: Directed Study in Politics
courses.description: Independent, in-depth study or research under faculty direction.

courses.name: Special Topics: Saving the World with Data
courses.description: Introduces contemporary political science research methods including fieldwork, interviews, surveys, statistical analysis, and AI applications.

courses.name: Special Topics: Politics and Society of Modern Japan
courses.description: Examines Japanese history and politics through Confucian thought, comparing traditional structures with contemporary challenges.

literary_cultural_studies.department: Department of History, Literature, and the Arts

courses.name: Introduction to Arts and Creative Industries
courses.description: Explores creativity through scholarship, creative practice, and creative industries study. Focuses on visual art, performing arts, or creative writing.

courses.name: Studies in Fiction
courses.description: Investigates various forms of narrative literature such as novels, short stories, and experimental narrative forms through theoretical and contextual analysis.

courses.name: Studies in Nonfiction
courses.description: Covers reading and research on nonfiction works including biographies, autobiographies, journalism, essays, film documentaries, and journals.

courses.name: Studies in Poetry
courses.description: Examines poetry as craft, exploring the relationship between strategic decisions poets make and the meanings derived through active reading.

courses.name: Introduction to Hip-Hop Culture
courses.description: Explores hip-hop's origins in West African traditions and American music, examining it as a significant cultural and political movement.

courses.name: Professional Writing
courses.description: Teaches written communication strategies across fields, focusing on adapting knowledge for diverse audiences with ethical dimensions.

courses.name: Introduction to Film Studies
courses.description: Introduces film language, examines relationships between movies and culture, analyzes cinematography, production design, editing, and narrative structure.

courses.name: Introduction to the Environmental Humanities
courses.description: Addresses how nature became separated from culture and examines ethical and political considerations for the environment and environmental justice.

courses.name: Women, Gender, and Sexuality Studies
courses.description: Applies critical lens to examine how gender and sexuality have been used as systems of social control and their roles in social change.

courses.name: Studies in Drama
courses.description: Focuses on dramatic literature in various forms with examination of representative works from diverse historical and cultural contexts.

courses.name: Introduction to Philosophy
courses.description: Studies core philosophical concepts, examining reasons for choices, actions, thoughts and beliefs using Western and non-Western sources.

courses.name: Introduction to Cultural Studies
courses.description: Examines cultural texts and practices, addressing questions about making culture and being made by culture.

courses.name: Introduction to World Music
courses.description: Builds vocabulary for describing music and studies cultures of music including aesthetics, traditions, values, and politics.

courses.name: Introduction to American Studies
courses.description: Introduces American Studies methodology and themes using literary texts, films, historical documents, music, visual art, and popular culture.

courses.name: Philosophy of Art
courses.description: Examines the history of aesthetic theory and philosophical issues raised by various art forms.

courses.name: Studies in Poetry (Advanced)
courses.description: Investigates poetry's power through craft analysis, examining strategic decisions poets make.

courses.name: Animation Theory, History, Practice
courses.description: Explores animation as vital component of film studies and visual culture across cinema, video games, and other media.

courses.name: Studies in Narrative
courses.description: Investigates various forms of narrative literature through theoretical and contextual study.

courses.name: Studies in Ethnic Literature of the United States
courses.description: Examines American literature from minority perspectives, exploring how other Americans brought their backgrounds and world views to national literature.

courses.name: Introduction to Studies in Jazz
courses.description: Introduces jazz as American art form through music theory, African/American/European cultural history, and recording industry examination.

courses.name: Music and Society
courses.description: Studies musicians and genres globally, examining ways music impacts communities, politics, and everyday life.

courses.name: Studies in Nonfiction (Advanced)
courses.description: Reading and research on nonfiction works across diverse non-fiction forms and genres.

courses.name: Studies in International Literature
courses.description: Focuses on interrelations between representative texts from different cultures, examining literature from particular regions in translated form.

courses.name: Topics in the Environmental Humanities
courses.description: Examines historical and contemporary relationships between human and more-than-human worlds using ecocriticism scholarship.

courses.name: British Literary Contexts: Beginnings to the Restoration
courses.description: Examines critical, social, cultural, and historical contexts of British literary production using canonical and non-canonical works.

courses.name: British Literary Contexts: Restoration to the Present
courses.description: Explores British literary production from the Restoration forward, examining how texts reflect and shape political and aesthetic values.

courses.name: American Literary Contexts: Beginnings to the Civil War
courses.description: Examines American literature from pre-contact through post-Civil War, analyzing how political tensions and social movements shape literary texts.

courses.name: American Literary Contexts: Civil War to the Present
courses.description: Explores post-Civil War American literature to present, examining influences that shape and are shaped by American literary texts.

courses.name: Poetry Writing Workshop
courses.description: Hands-on poetry creation through regular presentations of original writing with focus on form, revision, and contemporary publishing.

courses.name: Fiction Writing Workshop
courses.description: Hands-on story construction examining fundamental structure of fiction including character, plot, and theme through drafting and revision.

courses.name: Creative Writing Workshop
courses.description: Exploration of creative writing in specific genres with reading, extensive drafting, and critique.

courses.name: Modern Art in Europe 1880-1945
courses.description: Examines politics and practice of visual art movements in Europe including symbolism, expressionism, cubism, and surrealism.

courses.name: Global Art History Before 1850
courses.description: Chronological case studies exploring histories, interpretations, and reception of art and visual culture from prehistory to 1850.

courses.name: African American Studies
courses.description: Explores past and present experiences, cultures, and achievements of people of African descent in the United States.

courses.name: Asian American Studies
courses.description: Explores the interdisciplinary field through literary studies, cultural studies, and social movement history.

courses.name: Latin American Studies
courses.description: Examines Latin American and Latinx traditions, histories, and forms of cultural production.

courses.name: Native American Studies
courses.description: Interdisciplinary introduction to the history, culture, and contemporary experiences of Indigenous people in North America.

courses.name: American Studies
courses.description: Examines primary sources contributing to U.S. national culture formation with selective history of American writing.

courses.name: Sexuality and Culture
courses.description: Analyzes key texts from the canon of sexuality studies exploring 19th-century developments and new sexual paradigms.

courses.name: African Heritage in the Americas and Caribbean
courses.description: International perspective on the African Diaspora through cultural products including music, festivals, and religion.

courses.name: African Popular Culture
courses.description: Examines multiple forms of music, literature, and art in sub-Saharan Africa.

courses.name: Religious Studies
courses.description: Covers various religions, currently taught as introduction to Judaism through examination of traditional texts throughout Jewish history.

courses.name: Literary and Cultural Studies Internship
courses.description: Individually supervised work-study arrangements applying English arts and theory.

courses.name: Life and How to Live It
courses.description: Addresses fundamental questions about living and dying well through literary and philosophical works.

courses.name: The Philosophy of Technology
courses.description: Explores technology's nature and implications, providing historical overview and examining cultural, ethical, and political implications.

courses.name: Film Theory
courses.description: Approaches film as both art form and social practice, teaching key texts in film theory.

courses.name: Editing and Publishing Workshop: The Bryant Literary Review
courses.description: Mentorship in literary magazine editing through reviewing submissions and meeting publishing professionals.

courses.name: Contemporary Literature
courses.description: Examines new literary forms through reading recent literature exploring responses to changing social and cultural values.

courses.name: Ethics
courses.description: Introduces ethics and moral philosophy, applying ethical theories to concrete situations and contemporary issues.

courses.name: Anthropology of Music Industries
courses.description: Examines music industry as both business and creative site, exploring commodification and creativity.

courses.name: The Image of Business in Literature
courses.description: Insight into business through literary and cinematic perspectives, building bridges between humanities and business.

courses.name: Literature in a Historical Context
courses.description: Examines literary movements in-depth such as realism, modernism, romanticism, or gothic literature.

courses.name: Studies in Comparative Literature
courses.description: Analyzes literature within cross-cultural frameworks, examining development of a genre in an international context.

courses.name: Major Literary Figures
courses.description: Examines one writer or writing circle in-depth through biography, autobiography, letters, and critical reaction.

courses.name: Women and the Creative Imagination
courses.description: Considers women's cultural production through case studies exploring gendered identities and intersectionality of gender.

courses.name: Art and Politics in Nineteenth-Century France
courses.description: Focuses on late 19th-century French visual culture examining artists working from other places or identities.

courses.name: Studies in Graphic Narrative
courses.description: Studies comics and graphic novels as art form including autobiography, memoir, political documentary, and literary adaptation.

courses.name: Studies in Political Satire
courses.description: Examines the place of political satire within contemporary culture across television, film, stage, and print.

courses.name: Advanced Poetry Writing
courses.description: Continues poetry workshop with focus on deep revision and evolving language, requiring final portfolio.

courses.name: Sex, Love and Social Media
courses.description: Critically examines social media effects on friendship and intimacy through philosophical analysis.

courses.name: Cultural Studies Abroad
courses.description: Studies culture, history, and literature of a country or city including 10-12 day research trip.

courses.name: Critical and Cultural Theory
courses.description: Focuses on theoretical traditions shaping literary analysis from philosophy, social theory, linguistics, psychoanalysis, and gender studies.

courses.name: Creativity and Research Portfolio Workshop
courses.description: Develop portfolios for exhibition, performance, job applications, or graduate study.

courses.name: Directed Study in Literary and Cultural Studies
courses.description: Independent, in-depth study or research under faculty direction.

courses.name: Special Topics: Creative Writing Creative Field Trips
courses.description: Explores crucial decisions writers and artists make when engaging with the natural world through outdoor field trips.

courses.name: The Legal Environment of Business
courses.description: Examines nature of legal systems and processes including agency, contracts, UCC, debtor-creditor relationships, government regulation, and business entity selection.

courses.name: Introduction to the U.S. Legal System
courses.description: Overview of American legal framework covering sources of law, court systems, constitutional law, civil and criminal procedure, and regulatory structures.

courses.name: Global Legal Traditions
courses.description: Introduces comparative law study, examining how legal systems vary across nations including common law traditions.

courses.name: Criminal Law and Procedure
courses.description: Focuses on criminal responsibility, common crimes, and procedural requirements including rights to counsel, search warrants, and evidentiary rules.

courses.name: Civil Rights and Liberties
courses.description: Examines constitutional principles defining government limits and citizen rights, emphasizing Supreme Court decisions.

courses.name: Communications Law
courses.description: Studies media legal rights covering privacy, libel, defamation, copyright, regulatory agencies, and First Amendment theories.

courses.name: Law and the Digital World
courses.description: Addresses legal and policy issues regarding technology's societal impact including internet regulation and cyber threats.

courses.name: Law and Society
courses.description: Introduces law and society scholarship examining law's nature, legal actors' roles, and controversial issues.

courses.name: Sport and the Law
courses.description: Uses sports as a lens to examine societal change at multiple levels.

courses.name: International Law
courses.description: Covers state sovereignty, jurisdiction, treaty law, dispute resolution, international organizations, criminal law, force use, terrorism, and environmental law.

courses.name: Nonprofit Law and Governance
courses.description: Explores forming, maintaining, and governing tax-exempt organizations.

courses.name: Health Law
courses.description: Investigates legal regulation of health and healthcare industries.

courses.name: History, Law, and the Holocaust
courses.description: Explores Holocaust history and its impact on post-1945 international law development. Cross-listed with HIS 386.

courses.name: Legal Studies Internship
courses.description: Supervised employment applying legal studies theories.

courses.name: Markets and the Law: The Uniform Commercial Code
courses.description: Advanced examination of UCC provisions including contracts, sales, negotiable instruments, and secured transactions.

courses.name: Law of Financial Institutions
courses.description: Studies laws governing U.S. financial institutions including regulatory evolution and post-2008 crisis legislation.

courses.name: Gender and the Law
courses.description: Reviews historical and contemporary gender-related legal battles including voting, privacy, reproductive rights, and discrimination.

courses.name: Legal Ethics
courses.description: Investigates ethical systems guiding human thought, integrating ethical theories into law study.

courses.name: International Business Law
courses.description: Addresses government control of international business, comparing legal systems and covering international agreements.

courses.name: Seminar in Politics and Law
courses.description: Interdisciplinary capstone with in-depth examination and major research projects. Cross-listed with POLS 490.

courses.name: Directed Study in Legal Studies
courses.description: Independent study under faculty supervision.

courses.name: Student Success at Bryant University
courses.description: Introduces foundational concepts of higher education and equips students with strategies for success, emphasizing adaptability, accountability, resiliency, diversity, equity, and inclusion.

courses.name: Career Launch
courses.description: 15-week course developing career management competencies including personal career identity building and practical planning tools.

courses.name: Writing Workshop
courses.description: Students engage collaboratively as a writing community, emphasizing composition as an iterative process, exploring the potential of writing for social impact and change.

courses.name: Intercultural Communication
courses.description: Analyzes communication within intercultural contexts domestically and internationally, examining culture's effect on sending and interpreting messages.

courses.name: General Education Capstone
courses.description: Experiential course synthesizing general education skills with sustainable development goals and innovation methodology, tackling real-world problems with community partners.

theories_and_models.name: Shannon-Weaver Model (1948)
theories_and_models.definition: A linear model of communication consisting of sender, encoder, channel, decoder, receiver, and noise. Originally designed for telecommunications.
exam_topics: Difference between semantic and technical noise

theories_and_models.name: Berlo's SMCR Model (1960)
theories_and_models.definition: Expands Shannon-Weaver into Source-Message-Channel-Receiver, emphasizing that communication skills, attitudes, knowledge, social system, and culture of both source and receiver affect communication.

theories_and_models.name: Schramm's Interactive Model (1954)
theories_and_models.definition: Introduced the concept of shared field of experience between sender and receiver. Communication is only effective when both parties share overlapping experiences.

theories_and_models.name: Transactional Model
theories_and_models.definition: Both parties are simultaneously senders and receivers. Communication is a continuous, dynamic process influenced by noise, environment, and context.

theories_and_models.name: Uses and Gratifications Theory (1974)
theories_and_models.definition: Audiences actively seek out media to satisfy specific needs: information, personal identity, integration/social interaction, and entertainment.

theories_and_models.name: Agenda-Setting Theory (1972)
theories_and_models.definition: The media does not tell people what to think, but what to think about. Media coverage determines issue salience in public consciousness.
exam_topics: Real-world examples of media agenda setting

theories_and_models.name: Cultivation Theory (1976)
theories_and_models.definition: Long-term exposure to media (especially television) shapes viewers' perceptions of social reality, making heavy viewers more likely to perceive the world as portrayed on TV.

theories_and_models.name: Social Learning Theory / Social Cognitive Theory
theories_and_models.definition: People learn behaviors by observing others (models), including media figures. Includes attention, retention, reproduction, and motivation processes.

theories_and_models.name: Spiral of Silence (1974)
theories_and_models.definition: People who perceive their opinion as being in the minority tend to remain silent, which in turn reinforces the perception that the majority opinion is dominant.
exam_topics: How social media affects the spiral

theories_and_models.name: Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM) (1986)
theories_and_models.definition: Persuasion occurs through two routes: central (careful evaluation of arguments) and peripheral (reliance on cues like attractiveness or credibility).

key_concepts.definition: Speaker credibility and character; audience trust in the speaker's expertise and goodwill.
key_concepts.example: A doctor speaking about vaccines has high ethos.

key_concepts.definition: Emotional appeal; evoking feelings in the audience to persuade.
key_concepts.example: Charity ads showing suffering children use pathos.

key_concepts.definition: Logical appeal; using evidence, data, and reasoning to support claims.
The opportune moment; timeliness and appropriateness of the message.
key_concepts.example: Discussing climate policy after a natural disaster.

key_concepts.definition: 5-step persuasive speech structure: Attention, Need, Satisfaction, Visualization, Action.
key_concepts.example: Used in sales pitches and campaign speeches.

key_concepts.definition: Fear or anxiety about real or anticipated communication with others, especially public speaking.
Informative teaches/explains without advocating a position; persuasive seeks to change beliefs, attitudes, or behaviors.
key_concepts.example: Explaining how solar panels work vs. arguing we should use them.

key_concepts.definition: Process of examining demographics, psychographics, and situational factors of an audience to tailor a message.
key_concepts.example: Adjusting vocabulary for a professional vs. student audience.

key_concepts.definition: Relationships develop through gradual self-disclosure, moving from superficial to intimate layers (onion metaphor).
People seek information to reduce uncertainty about others during initial interactions.
Relationships involve ongoing tensions between contradictory desires: autonomy vs. connection, openness vs. closedness, predictability vs. novelty.
Kinesics (body movement), haptics (touch), proxemics (space), chronemics (time), vocalics/paralanguage (voice qualities), physical appearance.
Model for self-awareness showing 4 quadrants: Open, Blind, Hidden, Unknown areas of self.
People from different cultures manage conflict differently based on face concerns (self-face vs. other-face).
Process by which media professionals select, filter, and shape information before it reaches the public.
The merging of previously distinct media technologies, industries, and content onto digital platforms.
Consolidation of media outlets under fewer corporate owners, raising concerns about diversity of viewpoints.
How media presents information by emphasizing certain aspects while downplaying others, influencing interpretation.
Gap between those with access to digital technology and those without, often along socioeconomic, racial, and geographic lines.
Ability to access, analyze, evaluate, create, and act using all forms of communication.
Formal follows official channels (memos, meetings); informal includes grapevine, water-cooler talk.
Direction of message flow in organizational hierarchy.
Shared values, beliefs, and practices that characterize an organization.
Strategies for communicating during organizational crises. Includes Situational Crisis Communication Theory (SCCT).

major_perspectives.name: Behavioral Perspective
major_perspectives.core_idea: Behavior is learned through interaction with the environment. Only observable behavior should be studied scientifically.

key_concepts.definition: Learning through association. A neutral stimulus becomes associated with an unconditioned stimulus to produce a conditioned response.
key_concepts.example: Pavlov's dogs salivating at a bell.

key_concepts.definition: Learning through consequences. Behavior is strengthened by reinforcement or weakened by punishment.
key_concepts.example: A rat pressing a lever for food (Skinner box).

key_concepts.definition: Adding a desirable stimulus to increase behavior.
key_concepts.example: Giving a treat for good behavior.

key_concepts.definition: Removing an aversive stimulus to increase behavior.
key_concepts.example: Taking aspirin to remove a headache.

key_concepts.definition: Adding an aversive stimulus to decrease behavior.
Removing a desirable stimulus to decrease behavior.
Gradual weakening of a conditioned response when reinforcement stops.
Reinforcing successive approximations of a desired behavior.
Fixed-ratio, variable-ratio, fixed-interval, variable-interval. Variable-ratio produces highest, most consistent response rates.

major_perspectives.name: Cognitive Perspective
major_perspectives.core_idea: Mental processes (thinking, memory, perception, problem-solving) are central to understanding behavior.

key_concepts.definition: Mental framework for organizing and interpreting information.
Systematic errors in thinking (e.g., catastrophizing, all-or-nothing thinking, overgeneralization).
Mind works like a computer: encoding, storage, retrieval of information.
Sensorimotor (0-2), Preoperational (2-7), Concrete Operational (7-11), Formal Operational (11+).
Thinking about one's own thinking; awareness and regulation of cognitive processes.

exam_topics: Piaget's stages and what children can/cannot do at each

major_perspectives.name: Psychodynamic Perspective
major_perspectives.core_idea: Behavior is driven by unconscious desires, conflicts, and childhood experiences.

key_concepts.definition: Id: primitive drives (pleasure principle). Ego: mediator (reality principle). Superego: moral conscience (morality principle).
Unconscious strategies to protect ego from anxiety: repression, denial, projection, displacement, sublimation, rationalization, regression, reaction formation.
Freud's stages: Oral, Anal, Phallic, Latency, Genital. Fixation at any stage affects adult personality.

8 stages of development, each with a crisis: Trust vs. Mistrust, Autonomy vs. Shame/Doubt, Initiative vs. Guilt, Industry vs. Inferiority, Identity vs. Role Confusion, Intimacy vs. Isolation, Generativity vs. Stagnation, Integrity vs. Despair.
Repository of thoughts, memories, and desires below conscious awareness that influence behavior.
Jung's concept of shared, inherited unconscious containing archetypes common to all humans.

major_perspectives.name: Humanistic Perspective
major_perspectives.core_idea: Emphasis on free will, personal growth, and self-actualization. People are inherently good and motivated to reach their potential.

key_concepts.definition: 5-level pyramid: Physiological, Safety, Love/Belonging, Esteem, Self-Actualization. Lower needs must be met before higher ones.
Realizing one's full potential and capabilities.
Acceptance and support of a person regardless of what they say or do.
Rogers' approach: therapist provides empathy, genuineness, and unconditional positive regard to facilitate client growth.
An individual's belief about themselves, including attributes and who they are.

major_perspectives.name: Biological/Neuroscience Perspective
major_perspectives.core_idea: Behavior and mental processes have biological bases in genetics, brain structures, neurotransmitters, and evolution.

key_concepts.definition: Chemical messengers: serotonin (mood), dopamine (reward/motivation), norepinephrine (alertness), GABA (inhibition), acetylcholine (memory/muscle), glutamate (excitation).
Frontal lobe (planning/personality), temporal lobe (hearing/language), parietal lobe (sensation/spatial), occipital lobe (vision), limbic system (emotion/memory).
The brain's ability to reorganize and form new neural connections throughout life.
Studies on patients with severed corpus callosum showing hemispheric specialization.

major_perspectives.name: Sociocultural Perspective
major_perspectives.core_idea: Behavior is influenced by social and cultural context, including group dynamics, cultural norms, and social expectations.

key_concepts.definition: The gap between what a learner can do independently and what they can do with guidance.
Adjusting behavior to match group norms.
Complying with authority figures.
Temporary support provided to a learner to help them achieve a task beyond their current ability.
Systematic process: observation, hypothesis, experiment, analysis, conclusion.
The variable manipulated by the researcher.
The variable measured as the outcome.
An uncontrolled variable that may influence the DV.
Each participant has an equal chance of being in any condition, minimizing pre-existing differences.
Each member of the population has an equal chance of being selected for the study.
Measure of relationship between two variables (-1 to +1). Correlation does not imply causation.
Experiments manipulate variables to establish causation. Correlational studies measure relationships but cannot prove causation.
Neither participants nor researchers know who is in the experimental vs. control group, reducing bias.
Improvement caused by expectation of treatment rather than the treatment itself.
Consistency of a measure across time and observers.
Whether a test measures what it claims to measure.
Ethical requirement that participants understand and agree to the study's procedures and risks.
Key principles: beneficence, nonmaleficence, autonomy, justice, fidelity. IRB approval required for human subjects research.
Brief (0.5-3 sec) storage of sensory information. Iconic (visual) and echoic (auditory).
Limited capacity (7 +/- 2 items), duration ~20-30 seconds without rehearsal. Baddeley's model: phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, central executive, episodic buffer.
Unlimited capacity and potentially permanent. Types: explicit (declarative: episodic + semantic) and implicit (procedural, priming, conditioning).
Elaborative rehearsal, chunking, mnemonics, method of loci, spacing effect.
Decay theory (fading over time), interference (proactive = old disrupts new; retroactive = new disrupts old), retrieval failure (tip-of-the-tongue).
Most forgetting occurs rapidly after learning, then levels off. Spaced practice counteracts this.
Memory is not a perfect recording; it is actively reconstructed and can be influenced by schemas and post-event information.
Tendency to overestimate dispositional (personality) factors and underestimate situational factors when explaining others' behavior.
Attributing successes to internal factors and failures to external factors.
Discomfort from holding contradictory beliefs or behaving inconsistently with beliefs, motivating attitude change.
The more people present, the less likely any individual is to help in an emergency.
Group members prioritize consensus over critical thinking, leading to poor decisions.
Presence of others improves performance on simple tasks but impairs performance on complex tasks.
Individuals exert less effort in group settings than when working alone.
Prejudice = negative attitude. Stereotype = generalized belief. Discrimination = negative behavior toward a group.
Zimbardo's 1971 study showing how situational factors and assigned roles can dramatically influence behavior.
65% of participants administered maximum shocks when instructed by an authority figure, demonstrating the power of obedience.
Debate over whether genetics (nature) or environment (nurture) has greater influence on development.

Sensorimotor (0-2): object permanence. Preoperational (2-7): egocentrism, lack of conservation. Concrete operational (7-11): logical thinking about concrete events. Formal operational (11+): abstract reasoning.
Cognitive development occurs through social interaction and cultural tools. Zone of Proximal Development and scaffolding are central concepts.

Each stage involves a crisis: Trust/Mistrust (0-1), Autonomy/Shame (1-3), Initiative/Guilt (3-6), Industry/Inferiority (6-12), Identity/Role Confusion (12-18), Intimacy/Isolation (young adult), Generativity/Stagnation (middle adult), Integrity/Despair (late adult).
3 levels, 6 stages: Preconventional (obedience, self-interest), Conventional (conformity, law and order), Postconventional (social contract, universal ethics).
Bowlby: infants have innate need for attachment to a caregiver. Ainsworth's Strange Situation identified secure, avoidant, anxious-ambivalent, and disorganized attachment styles.
Demonstrated that infant monkeys preferred soft cloth surrogate mothers over wire mothers with food, showing contact comfort is crucial.
Environmental agents that can cause prenatal damage (e.g., alcohol causing Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders, drugs, viruses).
Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th ed., Text Revision. Standard classification used by mental health professionals in the US.
The 4 Ds: Deviance (differs from cultural norms), Distress (causes suffering), Dysfunction (impairs daily functioning), Danger (to self or others).
Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), Panic Disorder, Social Anxiety, Specific Phobias, Agoraphobia. Characterized by excessive fear and anxiety.
Major Depressive Disorder, Persistent Depressive Disorder (dysthymia). Symptoms include depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep/appetite changes, fatigue, guilt, suicidal ideation.
Alternating episodes of mania (elevated mood, energy, impulsivity) and depression. Bipolar I requires manic episode; Bipolar II requires hypomanic + depressive episodes.
Positive symptoms: hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech. Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition. Cognitive symptoms: impaired attention and memory.
Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: recurrent unwanted thoughts (obsessions) and repetitive behaviors (compulsions) performed to reduce anxiety.
Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: re-experiencing (flashbacks), avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, and hyperarousal following a traumatic event.
Mental disorders result from interaction between biological predisposition (diathesis) and environmental stressors.
Mental health involves biological (genetics, brain chemistry), psychological (thoughts, emotions), and social (relationships, culture) factors.

major_perspectives.name: Structural Functionalism
major_perspectives.core_idea: Society is a complex system whose parts work together to promote stability and solidarity. Each social institution serves a function.

key_concepts.definition: Intended, recognized consequences of a social institution.
Unintended, often unrecognized consequences.
key_concepts.example: Schools provide childcare while parents work.

key_concepts.definition: Element of society that disrupts social stability.
Durkheim's concept: values, norms, and structures that transcend individuals and constrain behavior.
Mechanical: social cohesion based on shared beliefs (simple societies). Organic: cohesion based on interdependence of specialized roles (complex societies).
Breakdown of social norms and values; normlessness leading to social instability.

exam_topics: Apply functionalism to education, religion, or family
Criticisms: ignores inequality, assumes stability is good

major_perspectives.name: Conflict Theory
major_perspectives.core_idea: Society is characterized by inequality and competition for scarce resources. Power dynamics between dominant and subordinate groups drive social change.

key_concepts.definition: Marx's classes: bourgeoisie own means of production; proletariat sell their labor.
Awareness of one's social class and economic interests, especially among the working class.
When the working class accepts the dominant ideology that serves ruling class interests.
Mills' concept: a small group of military, political, and corporate leaders who hold disproportionate power.
Class (economic), Status (prestige), Power (political influence). More nuanced than Marx's purely economic view.
Dominance of one group over others through cultural influence rather than overt force.

exam_topics: How does conflict theory explain education?
Criticisms: overemphasizes conflict, ignores social order

major_perspectives.name: Symbolic Interactionism
major_perspectives.core_idea: Society is constructed through everyday interactions and the meanings people attach to symbols, language, and gestures.

key_concepts.definition: Reality is created and maintained through social interaction.
We develop our self-concept based on how we believe others see us (imagine, interpret, evaluate).
Social life is like a theater: we perform roles on a front stage (public) and have a back stage (private) where we relax our performances.
Deviance is not inherent in an act but is defined by societal reaction and labels applied to individuals.
Mead: 'I' is the spontaneous, creative self. 'Me' is the socialized self that considers others' expectations.
Mead: significant others are specific people who influence us. Generalized other is the common expectations of society.
Thomas theorem: If people define situations as real, they are real in their consequences.

exam_topics: Apply Goffman's dramaturgy to social media

key_concepts.definition: Hierarchical arrangement of people in society based on wealth, power, and prestige.
Movement between social classes. Intragenerational (within one's lifetime) vs. intergenerational (compared to parents).
System where positions are based on individual achievement and talent rather than inherited status. Critiqued as an ideology masking structural inequality.
Overlapping social categories (race, class, gender, sexuality) create interconnected systems of advantage and disadvantage.
Non-financial social assets (education, speech, dress, knowledge) that promote social mobility.
Networks of relationships that provide resources and opportunities.
Government-set income threshold below which a family is considered poor. Absolute poverty vs. relative poverty.
Income: money earned (wages, salary). Wealth: total assets minus debts. Wealth inequality is much greater than income inequality.

culture.definition: Shared beliefs, values, norms, language, symbols, and material objects of a society.
Material: physical objects (tools, buildings). Non-material: ideas, values, norms.
Judging another culture by the standards of one's own culture.
Understanding a culture on its own terms without judging it by one's own standards.
A group within a larger culture with distinct values and behaviors (e.g., surfers, gamers).
A subculture that actively opposes dominant cultural norms (e.g., 1960s hippies, punk movement).
When material culture changes faster than non-material culture (e.g., technology outpacing laws).
Language shapes thought and perception of reality.

socialization.definition: Lifelong process of learning cultural norms, values, and behaviors.
Family (primary), peers, schools, media, religion, workplace.
Debate over biological inheritance vs. social environment in shaping behavior.
Primary: early childhood socialization within family. Secondary: later socialization in schools, workplaces, etc.
Radical alteration of personality through deliberate manipulation of environment (e.g., military boot camp, prison).
Places where people are cut off from broader society and controlled (prisons, mental hospitals, military).
Process by which individuals learn gender roles, expectations, and behaviors deemed appropriate for their sex.

deviance.definition: Behavior that violates social norms. Not necessarily criminal; socially constructed and varies by context.
Deviance results from the gap between culturally valued goals and legitimate means to achieve them. Modes of adaptation: conformity, innovation, ritualism, retreatism, rebellion.
Deviance is created by societal reaction. Primary deviance (initial act) vs. secondary deviance (adopting deviant identity after being labeled).
Deviance is learned through interaction with others who engage in deviant behavior.
Strong social bonds (attachment, commitment, involvement, belief) prevent deviance.
Non-violent crimes committed by individuals of high social status in their occupational roles.

key_concepts.definition: Supreme law of the United States, ratified 1788. Establishes government structure, powers, and individual rights. Based on popular sovereignty, limited government, separation of powers, checks and balances, and federalism.
Division of government into three branches: Legislative (Congress - makes laws), Executive (President - enforces laws), Judicial (Supreme Court - interprets laws).
Each branch can limit the power of the others. Examples: presidential veto, congressional override, judicial review, Senate confirmation of appointments.
