Division of power between national and state governments. Enumerated powers (federal), reserved powers (state), concurrent powers (shared).
Supreme Court's power to declare laws unconstitutional. Established in Marbury v. Madison (1803).

First 10 amendments to the Constitution guaranteeing individual liberties: speech, religion, press, assembly, petition (1st), bear arms (2nd), no unreasonable search (4th), due process (5th, 14th), speedy trial (6th), no cruel/unusual punishment (8th).
Civil liberties: protections FROM government (Bill of Rights). Civil rights: protections BY government against discrimination (14th Amendment, Civil Rights Act 1964).
System for electing the President. 538 electors; 270 needed to win. Each state gets electors equal to its congressional delegation.
Bicameral legislature: Senate (100, 2 per state, 6-year terms) and House of Representatives (435, proportional representation, 2-year terms).
Two-party system dominated by Democrats and Republicans. Functions: nominate candidates, mobilize voters, organize government, provide opposition.
Organizations that seek to influence policy on behalf of members. Use lobbying, campaign contributions (PACs/Super PACs), grassroots mobilization.

Marbury v. Madison (judicial review), McCulloch v. Maryland (federal supremacy), Brown v. Board (desegregation), Miranda v. Arizona (rights of accused), Roe v. Wade (privacy/abortion), Citizens United v. FEC (campaign finance).

theories.name: Realism
theories.core_idea: The international system is anarchic (no overarching authority). States are the primary actors, driven by self-interest and the pursuit of power. Conflict is inevitable.
variants: Classical realism (Morgenthau): human nature drives power-seeking
Neorealism/structural realism (Waltz): system structure determines behavior

theories.name: Liberalism/Idealism
theories.core_idea: Cooperation is possible through international institutions, trade interdependence, and democratic governance. Peace can be achieved through diplomacy and law.
variants: Neoliberal institutionalism (Keohane): institutions facilitate cooperation even under anarchy

theories.name: Constructivism
theories.core_idea: The international system is socially constructed. States' identities, interests, and behaviors are shaped by shared ideas, norms, and culture rather than being fixed.
key_concepts: Anarchy is what states make of it (Wendt)

theories.name: Marxism/Critical Theory
theories.core_idea: Global politics is driven by economic forces and class struggle. The international system reflects and reinforces capitalist exploitation.
key_concepts: Imperialism as highest stage of capitalism

key_concepts.definition: A state's supreme authority within its territory and independence from external interference.
Modern state system originating from the Peace of Westphalia (1648), establishing principles of state sovereignty and non-interference.
When one state increases military capability for defense, other states perceive it as a threat and also arm, leading to an arms race.
Preventing aggression by threatening unacceptable retaliation, especially in nuclear strategy (MAD: Mutually Assured Destruction).
Soft power: influence through culture, values, and institutions. Hard power: coercion through military or economic force.
Increasing interconnectedness of economies, cultures, and politics across national borders.
International organization (est. 1945) with Security Council (5 permanent members with veto), General Assembly, and specialized agencies.
Emphasis on individual rights, limited government, free markets, rule of law. Key thinkers: John Locke, Adam Smith, John Stuart Mill.
Supports government intervention to promote equality and social welfare while protecting civil liberties.
Values tradition, social order, authority, and gradual change. Skeptical of rapid reform. Key thinkers: Edmund Burke, Russell Kirk.
Advocates collective/government ownership of means of production to reduce inequality. Democratic socialism vs. authoritarian socialism.
Marx and Engels: class struggle drives history; capitalism will be replaced by a classless society. Communism: abolition of private property, state withers away.
Ultranationalist, authoritarian ideology emphasizing state power, militarism, racial superiority, and rejection of democracy. Historical examples: Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy.
Maximizes individual liberty and minimizes government in both economic and social spheres.
Political approach appealing to ordinary people against a perceived corrupt elite. Can be left-wing or right-wing.
Identification with and devotion to one's nation, often including belief in national superiority.
Advocacy for political, economic, and social equality of the sexes. Waves: 1st (suffrage), 2nd (workplace/reproductive rights), 3rd (intersectionality), 4th (digital activism).
Political movement advocating environmental protection through government regulation, sustainability, and addressing climate change.

key_points: Jamestown (1607) first permanent English settlement
Plymouth Colony (1620) Pilgrims/Mayflower Compact
13 colonies: New England, Middle, Southern
Mercantilism and Navigation Acts
Great Awakening (religious revival 1730s-1740s)
Stamp Act (1765), Boston Massacre (1770), Boston Tea Party (1773)
Declaration of Independence (1776) - Thomas Jefferson
Key battles: Lexington/Concord, Saratoga, Yorktown
Treaty of Paris (1783)
Enlightenment influences: Locke (natural rights), Montesquieu (separation of powers)
Articles of Confederation (weak central government)
Constitutional Convention (1787) - Great Compromise, 3/5 Compromise
Federalists (Hamilton) vs. Anti-Federalists (Jefferson)
Washington's presidency, precedent-setting
Hamilton's financial plan, national bank debate
Louisiana Purchase (1803)
Indian Removal Act (1830), Trail of Tears
Manifest Destiny - belief in continental expansion
Mexican-American War (1846-1848)
Gold Rush (1849)
Cotton gin (1793) expanded slavery
Missouri Compromise (1820)
Compromise of 1850, Fugitive Slave Act
Uncle Tom's Cabin (Harriet Beecher Stowe)
Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857)
Abolitionists: Frederick Douglass, William Lloyd Garrison, Sojourner Truth
Secession of Southern states, Confederate States of America
Key battles: Bull Run, Antietam, Gettysburg, Vicksburg, Sherman's March
Emancipation Proclamation (1863)
13th Amendment (abolished slavery), 14th (equal protection), 15th (voting rights)
Reconstruction: Freedmen's Bureau, Black Codes, Jim Crow laws
Transcontinental Railroad (1869)
Captains of Industry/Robber Barons: Carnegie, Rockefeller, Vanderbilt, Morgan
Labor movement: AFL (Gompers), Knights of Labor, strikes
Immigration waves (Ellis Island)
Progressive Era reforms: muckrakers, trust-busting, women's suffrage
WWI: US enters 1917, Wilson's 14 Points, Treaty of Versailles, League of Nations rejected
Roaring Twenties, Great Depression (1929), New Deal (FDR)
WWII: Pearl Harbor (1941), D-Day, atomic bombs on Hiroshima/Nagasaki
Holocaust, Nuremberg Trials
UN established (1945), beginning of Cold War
Containment policy, Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan
Korean War, Vietnam War, Cuban Missile Crisis
McCarthyism and Red Scare
Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
Rosa Parks, Montgomery Bus Boycott
Martin Luther King Jr., March on Washington, 'I Have a Dream'
Civil Rights Act (1964), Voting Rights Act (1965)
Assassination of JFK (1963), MLK and RFK (1968)
Mesopotamia: Sumerians, Code of Hammurabi, cuneiform
Egypt: pharaohs, pyramids, Nile civilization
Greece: democracy (Athens), philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle), Alexander the Great
Rome: republic to empire, Roman law, Pax Romana, fall of Western Roman Empire (476 CE)
Monotheism: Judaism, Christianity, Islam
Feudalism: lords, vassals, serfs, manorialism
Catholic Church as dominant institution, papal authority
Crusades (1096-1291): religious wars, cultural exchange, trade expansion
Black Death (1347-1351): killed ~1/3 of European population
Magna Carta (1215): foundation of constitutional government
Rebirth of classical learning, humanism
Art: Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael
Literature: Machiavelli (The Prince), Shakespeare
Spread from Italy across Europe
Invention of printing press (Gutenberg, c. 1440)
Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517)
Protestant denominations: Lutheran, Calvinist, Anglican
Counter-Reformation: Council of Trent, Jesuits
Religious wars: Thirty Years' War (1618-1648)
Peace of Westphalia (1648): modern state system
Copernicus: heliocentric model. Galileo: telescope observations. Newton: laws of motion/gravity
Scientific method development
Enlightenment thinkers: Locke (natural rights, social contract), Montesquieu (separation of powers), Voltaire (free speech), Rousseau (general will)
Impact on American and French Revolutions
Causes: fiscal crisis, Enlightenment ideas, social inequality (3 estates)
Storming of the Bastille (1789), Declaration of the Rights of Man
Reign of Terror (Robespierre)
Napoleon's rise, conquests, Napoleonic Code, defeat at Waterloo (1815)
Congress of Vienna (1815): restored conservative order
Began in Britain (late 1700s), spread to Europe and US
Key innovations: steam engine, spinning jenny, factory system, railways
Urbanization, child labor, harsh working conditions
Rise of capitalism (Adam Smith) and socialism (Marx/Engels: Communist Manifesto 1848)
Labor movements, unions, reform legislation
European scramble for Africa (Berlin Conference 1884-85)
British Empire, French colonies, Belgian Congo
Social Darwinism, 'White Man's Burden' as justifications
Resistance movements across colonized regions
Legacy: borders, ethnic conflicts, underdevelopment
World War I (1914-1918): alliance system, trench warfare, Treaty of Versailles
Russian Revolution (1917), rise of Soviet Union
Rise of fascism: Mussolini (Italy), Hitler (Germany), militarism (Japan)
World War II (1939-1945): Holocaust, atomic age
Cold War (1947-1991): US vs. USSR, proxy wars, nuclear arms race, fall of Berlin Wall (1989)
